headline: CATTLE HIT 52-WEEK HIGH AS CRUDE EXPLODES
subheadline: Live cattle reached $253.45 while crude oil surged 4% overnight on Middle East tensions.
lead: Live cattle hit a 52-week high at $253.45, up 1.8%, extending Monday's breakout call to an eighth straight session above $245. The funds are backing the move with fresh length as crude oil exploded 4% overnight to $103.34, dragging energy costs higher just as planting season hits the critical May window. Yesterday's cattle breakout call is paying off, but the energy surge changes the input cost equation for producers racing against calendar pressure.
teaser: Cattle reach new highs while crude oil's 4% surge reshuffles the cost structure for spring planting.

one_number.context: Live cattle reached their highest level in a full year, confirming the breakout momentum that started eight sessions ago. The funds are adding length at the highs, not selling into strength. Feeder cattle at $373.07 tells you the replacement cost story is intact through summer.

yesterdays_call.summary: Called pricing remaining summer cattle on the breakout above $245 for three sessions.
yesterdays_call.note: Eight sessions above $245 and a 52-week high validates the directional call with room to run.

sections.title: Cattle Extend Breakout
sections.body: Live cattle hit $253.45, a 52-week high, extending the breakout to eight sessions above $245. Managed money is adding length at the highs, not selling into strength like they usually do at range extremes. Feeder cattle at $373.07 confirms replacement costs are climbing faster than finished cattle, compressing margins for backgrounders. The seasonal demand pattern into Memorial Day is working, but the real driver is shrinking inventories meeting steady export demand. Cash basis in Nebraska is talking loud, premiums to futures widening for the third week.
sections.bottom_line: Breakout extending with fund buying backing the move higher.
sections.farmer_action: Hold remaining fed cattle inventory — momentum is accelerating with fund support.

sections.title: Crude Oil Explodes Higher
sections.body: WTI crude spiked 4% overnight to $103.34 on renewed Middle East tensions, dragging diesel and fertilizer costs higher just as Corn Belt planting hits the critical window. The energy complex is repricing geopolitical risk after three weeks of complacency, and that flows straight through to operating costs for producers. Natural gas held steady at $2.66, but crude's move changes the diesel equation for spring fieldwork. The timing couldn't be worse — May is peak fuel usage month for planting operations.
sections.bottom_line: Energy costs spiking at worst possible time for planting operations.

sections.title: Grains Mark Time
sections.body: Corn gained 0.4% to $4.77, soybeans added 0.2% to $11.94, and wheat jumped 1.1% to $6.63 in quiet trade. The action is in spreads, not outright prices — December corn at $4.98 versus nearby at $4.77 shows carry working, but the 21-cent inverse is narrower than yesterday. Managed money is neutral in corn, net long 64,000 contracts in soybeans, waiting for weather or export news. Tuesday's planting progress report showed 35% of corn planted, right on the five-year average. The trade is pricing planting risk, not planting reality.
sections.bottom_line: Spreads more interesting than outright prices as planting proceeds on schedule.

spread_to_watch.label: Live cattle / feeder cattle ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: Feeder cattle outpacing live cattle tells you replacement costs are climbing faster than finished cattle prices. The narrowing ratio from 0.685 last week signals margin compression for backgrounders. When this ratio drops below 0.67, it usually means the cattle cycle is turning.

basis.headline: Corn basis firming in Eastern Belt as ethanol demand returns
basis.body: Eastern Corn Belt basis is firming 3-5 cents as ethanol plants come back online after spring maintenance. Indiana and Ohio elevators are paying premiums for the first time since February. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with the seasonal pattern as river transportation opens up.

weekly_thread.question: Can cattle momentum extend above $250 with energy costs spiking?
weekly_thread.status_text: Energy costs exploding higher changes the input equation, but cattle hit $253.45 anyway. The breakout momentum is stronger than the headwind so far.

the_more_you_know.title: Why 52-Week Highs Change Everything
the_more_you_know.body: Live cattle at $253.45 just hit its 52-week high, but that's not just a psychological level. Technical funds use 52-week breakouts as primary entry signals, which explains the fresh buying at apparent resistance. Unlike daily breakouts, 52-week highs attract systematic money that holds positions for months, not days. The last time cattle cleared a 52-week high was July 2023, and the subsequent rally lasted 11 weeks.

daily_quote.text: To forget how to dig the earth and to tend the soil is to forget ourselves.

source_summary: CME futures, USDA reports, energy markets
